catching data (in the style of Perry Bible Fellowship)

2 min read
Just now

--

Things have changed and stayed the same.
Good engineers have good mental models. True before. More true now.
You are now limited by your own cognitive contextual limits and how fast you can learn. Before LLMs, there was the hard part, and there was the easy part.
The hard part was the figuring out. The designing, the going back and forth with colleagues about trade offs and and constraints. This has always been the programming part. This part is very much still true, possibly more so because it now involves a machine intelligence faster than us.
The easy part was the writing code. Headphones on and just go. It was relaxing. It was great.
The easy part is gone. And that presents a problem for some. The act of typing out code, for many, was the act of thinking. Coding was the act of working through ideas, and unlocking understanding. But it was also the conflation of planning and execution. It led to coding by exception, logically pigeon holing yourself, creating stateful traps and many, many other issues we find in any codebase.
There were always anti-patterns telling us this was not a great method, but not everyone made themselves aware.
I was recently asked what I actually do now. It was surprisingly hard to answer. I am busier than ever, but touching code is a small portion of my activity. It’s like I am constantly loading context and reasoning about direction, constraint, and interest, then deciding. The hard part over and over again.
Because these machines are so quick and so confident about their answers, it becomes work to ensure the right concerns are weighted at the right level, such that the outcome is useful in a way that does not establish an extension trajectory that will make issues, or ignore a user persona’s actual need.
And then there is the cruft. These machines are addative to a codebase in a way that treats the entire past as a good idea and golden truth. And any bit of text not in the context simply does not exist. Over a long enough timescale, this produces insane results that will be justified and reinforced by the LLM.
I find that I need to protect the program model from unnecessary extension and interleaving of behaviors. Architecture, rules, and skills help with this, but again, the past is The Truth to these things and knowing when an idea has lost it’s value and to change tack does not seem to be in the cards here.
The human is still responsible for the outcome, and the increase in speed has increased the demand for planning, organizing, and deciding. And not just for code, but for intent, infrastructure, logical architecture, and deployment architecture.
It’s exhausting.
